reviews.review_text:
This game was magical start to finish. A rollercoaster of emotion, I cried, I laughed, I got frustrated at certain situations, I cried some more.

It's hard to find a game that makes you feel more at home than this. I would say that anyone who plays this will relate to one of the characters in some way - and that makes it hurt even more... but in a way that makes you cry with a smile on your face.

If you want a story based game with easy (but occasionally grindy) gameplay, look no further. But man, bring some tissues, you're going to need them. Enjoy the game, and thank you to the devs for making such a masterpiece in storytelling that hits home harder than any game I've played in a long time.

[h2]It was amazing.[/h2]
Spiritfarer is a philosophical story about the lives of people who were close to the heroine, but their stories can be so close to other real people. 

The game itself is extremely leisurely and might even seem boring, but that's its main advantage: like a meditation, it forces you to slowly explore its world, unhurriedly telling its story. 

Most of the game consists of farming and parkour, and you will need to monitor the mood of the spirits by fulfilling their wishes. 

The game is beautifully drawn, with the animal-shaped spirits and the eternal gates truly captivating. Music is a special kind of art: flutes, violins and piano create a magical feeling of calm, and each spirit has its own unique theme.

Stella herself is an incredibly charming character, despite her silence. You become invested in her, and the ending truly brings tears to your eyes. 

It's now one of my favorite games, even though it's not as extensive in gameplay as Stardew Valley or other similar games. I definitely recommend diving into this world if you like meditative gameplay and music that relaxes your mind.

Spiritfarer is by far my favorite game of all time, and I cannot recommend it enough. It’s the sort of game where every mechanic connects to one another seamlessly, and the narrative and themes are not only beautiful, but are reinforced by the game’s design.

The core gameplay loop of Spiritfarer is simple; you sail your boat around a large world populated by various islands, pick up spirits onto your boat, and fulfil their requests, which lead you to new islands, new buildings to construct on your boat, and new spirits to take on board. As the requests of spirits lead you further through the game, they gradually stop holding your hand, and you are encouraged to sail around and find new islands and spirits on your own. The requests ensure that you’re never completely lost, but they aren’t timed or limited in any way; the game lets you move at your own pace and experience the world how you want.

Most of the resource-gathering of the game is facilitated by simple minigames. Some of the top negative reviews of this game rightfully mention the tedium of these tasks as something that drags down the game experience. For some, that is the case. However, in my opinion, I think it’s very intentional that Spiritfarer forces you to slow down. In a world of fast-paced action games and rapidly shrinking attention spans, Spiritfarer invites you into its world and only asks that you take your time experiencing it. And personally, the bit of skill, precision, and learning that is involved with the minigames is just engaging enough that the monotonous tasks feel fun. Sure, if you need to cut 50 logs in a row, it’s going to suck a little bit, but there’s no rush to do that. You can explore, gather other resources you need, complete a different request, experiment with meals you can cook, talk to your spirits and hear about their stories, and more. What I love most about Spiritfarer is that there is always something to do. The game isn’t trying to entertain you, it’s trying to immerse you, and with that in mind I believe the mechanics are perfectly executed.

While the building blocks of the game are solid, they really shine when combined with one another. As you play through the game, your ship gets larger and you have more room to cram in buildings, houses, gardens, and improvements. Not only do you consistently have to navigate your ship to process your resources, making layout important, but you also have to fit the irregularly shaped buildings in a space that is often too small, somewhat like Tetris. And, to top it off, several events have you run around your ship collecting resources. Plotting your route to encounter these events to gather the resources you need is important. When you encounter them, the placement of your buildings, along with various utilities that let you move about your ship with ease, and the movement abilities you unlock throughout your journey, dictate how easy or difficult obtaining the resources is; a well-designed ship lets you deal with most events easily, while a messy ship might mean you have to find and complete the event a second time. But, the movement abilities aren’t just useful on your ship; most every island has secrets and collectables that you need later-game abilities to access, meaning there’s almost always a reason to backtrack and keep exploring.

So, the mechanics harmonize together to create a dynamic experience. However, the very best part of Spiritfarer, in my opinion, is the interconnection between the mechanics and the game’s narrative. Taken at face value, for every spirit, you find them, complete some tasks to get them to join you on your boat, tackle their requests, and then finally release them at the Everdoor. Throughout their requests, you’ll learn about their stories, lives, and deaths from bits of dialogue and little interactions. However, when I first played Spiritfarer, I felt like there just was not enough. I saw this comment on a few negative reviews: the characters don’t feel developed enough. This is the one “flaw” with Spiritfarer that I now always reject. Spiritfarer is a game about death. By design, you will never know its characters well enough. They will leave you faster than you expected, and you will miss them when they are gone. As a younger person who didn’t quite understand death, I thought you should always get to learn everything about every character. But that is not how life works; death and regret walk hand-in-hand, and few people leave our lives without pain. Spiritfarer, in this way, is brutally realistic. Its characters are human, flawed, rude, mean, unkind, and uncomfortable. But they are also kind, and real, and have reasons for their behavior. You’re not forced to like every character, but you do have to facilitate their requests. When I first played this game, I hated some of the spirits, and to be frank, some of them still piss me off. But as I played, I understood them a bit more, and that level of character complexity is a very rare find. But I mentioned the mechanics relating to the narrative, and so far, I’ve just discussed the narrative. Unfortunately, I can’t clarify too much on that point without spoiling the natural understanding of the spirits’ stories that’s so vital to the authentic Spiritfarer experience. However, what I can mention is that everything outside of the direct narrative says something about the spirits. Their food preferences, the events they unlock, the buildings they teach you how to use, the things that make them happy and sad, and even the way their questlines are formatted. Spiritfarer breaks its own formula to tell its story, even in the simplest of ways. Most spirits come onto your ship first, then ask for a house afterwards. However, a certain spirit doesn’t want to impose, and opts to not go on your ship until you take it upon yourself to build them a house. Another spirit never asks for a house at all, and just takes up residence in another’s! Almost every character deviates from the rest in some slight way that makes all their stories feel unique. If you think any character in this game is boring, I am of the opinion that you should play the game a second time and see if your opinion changes- mine certainly did.

I could go on for probably thousands more words on why I love Spiritfarer so much, and thousands more if I wasn’t bound by spoilers. However, this review is already getting far too long. All I’ll say is that Spiritfarer is a magical sort of a game. It creeps up on you with its deceptively simple start, and then somewhere along the ride you wonder where you started caring so much. It is the game that taught me how to cope with death, as silly as it sounds. While I think everyone should play this game, no matter their life experience, if you have struggled with grief and death, this game is a must-play on no uncertain terms. If you have a soul and want to experience one of the very best narratives I have ever found, I beseech you, play this game.

Words cannot sum up how special this game is. The characters and their story's resonated with me so much, especially Gustav and Astrid. The game is pretty deep and philosophical, it's themes of end of life and stuff can be a lot to handle, but it makes the game so much more personal and valuable to the player.  "It's enough to make a grown man cry, and that's ok" - Cop Guy

If you want a review on gameplay mechanics or other technical crap, pass this review by, because while this game is truly enjoyable in every aspect, that is not what mattered to me nor what I wish to celebrate about it.

This game... is so much more than a game. Its reflection, sadness, joy, and pure catharsis rolled into a beautiful and melodious experience. I have faced my own demons and sorrow about someone who was extremely close to me while helping other souls find solace in death. While I will forever have sadness when I think of her, it has helped me find peace in the thought that maybe she is looking at me with love and forgiveness.

If you want just a cozy game, there is a multitude of titles you can choose for that, but if you want that and you want to face emotions that may not be the most comfortable, but chances are you will endure, absolutely play this game. It has helped shape my perspective and feelings towards life and death and with that has earned a special place in my heart.

A game about hugging and taking care of furries who died and are now in limbo. Feed them their favorite meals, grant requests, build them a house, etc. all on your giant ship. Then when you become great friends, take them to a deserted place and ice them in the back of the head (they go on to the actual afterlife and you never see them again). Yeah you're basically the taxi to the farm upstate.

This game is incredible. It made a grown man cry and I haven't been able to in years. This game gave me lessons to keep in life and I loved having a change in pace from just first person shooters. 10/10

Great storytelling and moving plot. If you get it on sale, it's a great value.
Grinding can be tiring, especially before the quick travel become available - even then it is tiring.
But finishing the game is satisfying, and greatly recommended.

I'm surprised, and saddened honestly, to say I didn't like this game; it failed completely to live up to its reputation. I thought, given the visuals and tone, this game was going to be a masterpiece (like GRIS in particular). However, I leave this game extremely disappointed and unable to even finish it.

My biggest issue is that this game's too tedious. The management is simple and engaging enough at first but lacks any depth to be interesting over this game's ~20h runtime. After all, I can only do the same things in the same way so many times before it becomes mind-numbing. Moreover, no two characters feel distinct either; they look different but are all functionally identical. There aren't any characters who force you to engage with the game's systems in novel ways, creating a predictable and menial loop. Well, there is one character that feels distinct, but only because they're insufferable. 

The amount of backtracking is also beyond excessive. I'm a sucker for discovery, so the early game was engaging, but it just kept demanding you backtrack constantly. Repeatedly you spend waiting in transit, only to revisit the same place again and again. Mind you, backtracking isn't innately bad, but here it is because the ship's speed is so sluggish that you spend most time just waiting. The fast travel system is also woefully insufficient; It makes no sense why the game’s pacing slows down as the world expands. More of the map unlocked means more distance to travel, but the game never gives adequate speed upgrades to compensate, which makes it drag as you spend longer doing nothing but waiting. Plus, it baffles me as to why they would make the final Bow upgrades the most impactful speed ones. Why give players the quality of life of shorter wait times at the very end of the experience? Not being able to navigate at night was also extremely annoying, and the game never justifies its existence as a mechanic.

I will admit, though, that this game isn't all bad. The visuals and animations are gorgeous, and it's evident how much love and effort went into this; the soundtrack is also really strong. I know there is a great game in here somewhere because I was enjoying it at the start, but it shouldn't be as long as it is. This is a twelve-hour game at best, not twenty plus.
Cozy game about managing a ship and ferrying souls to the afterlife.

It's very annoying when a game claims to be cozy while it's core mechanisms keeps building more tasks for you to complete and punish you when you stop. Your passengers will grow hungry overtime and repetitively demand their needs to be met. It's like being forced to switch between short videos when you expect a nature documentary. 
Also, for a claimed cozy game, a few passengers' stories surely involved political content and can be a bit controversial.
Gentle, engrossing story, beautiful graphics, clear controls and goals

This game blends story and gameplay beautifully. It helped me work through the grief of losing my grandparents. I highly recommend this game to anyone who like resource collection, story telling, or just lost a loved one.
A lot of fun! Playful, engaging, really beautiful to look at and a touching story on top of it all. Great game.

I'm nowhere near the end of the game but I'm having a wonderful time playing it. It touches on so many emotions and I have found myself getting quite upset over several characters and their departures. Truly a one of a kind game that I find very difficult to put down once I get started. It's crazy how attached you get to characters in such a small amount of time! Beautiful music, art design and stories!!

Very relaxing and enjoy doing the tasks and side tasks as well, pretty animation and the story is nice~
I've only done the 15% but I enjoy that I can take my time on this game.

Yes please go in blind, no YouTube videos no guides nothing.

The game hurts in the best way possible. You'll be bonding with these people some even closer to you. Enjoying their company, listening to their stories, and help their requests you will forget that your helping for a important reason. Right until the moment you finish their last request and have to say goodbye.

I haven't finished or play in 5 yrs yet saying goodbye still hurt.

May have cried - multiple times. 

Super fun game with really nice graphics and cute NPC's to guide to the everdoor. Fulfilling and heartbreaking all at the same time.

This game is amazing. It has become one of my favorite games of all time. The writing is so well done and the game is absolutely beautiful. I know I was supposed to learn how to move on but I did not really do that it is so hard to say goodbye to the spirts. Definitely a must play game for those who love cozy games.

I first played this on my Switch, but it feels like a difference experience playing on PC and Steam Deck. i recommend having tissues near by because this game is amazing and there are some tearjerking moments in it too.

What starts as a simple cozy game starts getting emotional pretty quick. 35 hours now, and there is still some content left by the time I finished the game. At the final moments, the way Stella's story opens up was amazing. A great metaphor about death and loss. At times, it feels repetitive, but overall, a decent game.
It’s a very touching game. It’s sad, but beautiful. Very story rich, and by far one of the best and most memorable games I’ve played.

This is an absolutely lovely game with a lot of deep storylines and a million side quests. The main storyline alone should take you over 60 hours to complete. The graphics are gorgeous, and the animations feel really smooth. Each character has such a unique story to them, with all of them being related to you in some way. As you go along, you find out more about your life before you died and the spirit world around you. Overall, it is very deep and immersive, definitely a 10/10!
Such a bittersweet game about letting go, so many of the characters made me cry when I had to bring them to the Everdoor.

It's a sweet game that has real personality. The art and animations are all fun and the whole package oozes charisma.

People say it's helped them accept loss and process grief and I can see why. It's a touching game about life and death and love and regret and hopes and dreams; All valuable parts of the human experience that we share and are a part of until we're not. For us older millennials who have started to lose our parents or some friends or siblings.. It really makes us think back on those people, doesn't it?



I never finished. I Didn't feel the need to. Honestly, the gameplay loop bored me.
Simply a beautiful game. Relaxed, creative, and delightfully unhurried.
Beautiful storytelling and very cozy. But be prepared to feel your feelings and process grief.

+ MC’s story
+ [b] main theme [/b]
+ exploring the islands
+ cat companion
+ a tiny berserk reference

+/− visual style and gameplay

− boring minigames

Overall, there are more positives than negatives, so I recommend it. Maybe a co-op playthrough would be more enjoyable (and faster).

The themes surrounding grief and loss are impressively done. There are at least a few stories that all people can relate to, and the tasks in the game are well paced, pushing you to progress without feeling out of your depth.

I can't remember the last time I played a game with this much heart; I was constantly invested in the story and wanted to help out every spirit I met, which made them passing on incredibly bittersweet every time. Mechanically, I did feel like the game got a little repetitive toward the end, but I always wanted to push forward to see the next story beat. Great game, will hit you in the feels!

A great cozy game which never gets boring because there's always something to do. The storylines are amazing and I love the character designs. Can't wait to see more of them and experience more of the game as I play on.
Beautiful game design, the artwork and music are stunning. The story telling is deeply moving, I've played through it a few times and am still brought to tears by some of the developments.

A beautiful and thoughtful game about life and death and family. A bit of a collect-o-thon at times, and it feels a little frontloaded with the best characters, though a few of the later characters are delightful. Gorgeous design and aesthetic, very fun gameplay, great writing, at times funny and quirky and at times devastatingly sad. All in all, a very unique game that I enjoyed pretty much every minute of.

Very relaxing and cozy game with old disney vibe graphics and deep storylines of characters of their past lives on earth, representing real life problems that people go through. Game is best for people with a sense of empathy bc it would make it easier to understand characters perspective, and for people who just want to slow down and chill. Game also has an amazing soundtrack that can really calm a person down. Very recommended ❤

I have mixed feelings about this game. I can tell why it is so loved by so many, but by the end of the game I felt a little discontented.

Pros:
-Adorable animations, lovely colors, and fun areas to explore
-Unique characters that quickly invested me in their stories, and left me genuinely heartbroken and teary eyed at points
-Expert storytelling for deep and challenging topics
-Generally fun gameplay, including crafting, navigating and exploring, building, farming, platforming, and more.

Cons:
-The sailing part is SLOW. So very, very slow. It was fine at first when I needed to mine resources while sailing, but by the end it was a slog to get through. There are fast track options meant to save you time, but they have the same issues I have with other cozy games like Animal Crossing where even the "shortcuts" are slow. Made for a tough time the last 10 hours or so of gameplay, especially when it is basically all you are doing for hours on end.
-The ending was not satisfying for me. I felt so emotionally drained by assisting the other characters (in a good way) and then the final interaction just felt like nothing in comparison. In another game that didn't invest so much into its storytelling the rest of the time, this wouldn't have bothered me. It's not a bad ending by any means, but it isn't amazing. And that is only noticeable because the rest of the game was so good that the finale having a drop in quality was noticeable and impactful in a way that left me dissatisfied.

Overall I enjoyed 3/4ths of my time playing it. And it is incredibly lovingly crafted by a clearly passionate team, so I can't do anything but recommend it. I only wish the ending was a bit stronger than it turned out to be.

Purchased on the strength of a demo. It's a tough game based on subject matter as it's about end of life stuff. It is done in such a nice "cozy" way however. The game never pressures you hard to complete stuff, it's all about going at your own pace.

It may hit you hard emotionally. I know every trip to the Everdoor got my eyes watering. Maybe my room is too dusty, I don't know. 

Anyway, definitely recommend this.

One of the best "cozy" games out there. Definitely makes you feel something as you (and your spirits) progress. If anybody is on the fence about whether or not they want to play this game, just do it. It is well-worth your time and money. You will understand when you let the game happen to you.

I also 100% it in 1 run (I did the save-scumming for the choosing options achievements), which didn't feel like a slog at all and it honestly made it more enjoyable to be able to, not just have to complete my spirits requests, but also to have to make all of them ecstatic at one point or another before moving on (for the rarest achievement). Would definitely recommend trying to do this if you want to, and I would recommend against using a guide if possible (up until you can't do it yourself).

I bought this game because I thought the cat was cute.
The game being good was the cherry on top.
100/10. 
Recommend to those who enjoy Stardew Valley or other cozy games.

Spiritfarer is such an emotionally rich, peaceful, and cosy game. I'm not typically into games like this, so it was a leap of faith. I originally played the demo on my Switch and decided to purchase the PC version on Steam.
When I played Spiritfarer, I was recovering from a major surgery for cancer. With a related and newfound visual impairment, I needed to play something chill. I had no idea that I was signing up for a truly moving and profoundly beautiful experience. 
When I look back on that difficult period of my life, I think so fondly about my experience with this game, and the world it whisked me away to. It helped me process my mortality while giving me comfort and enrichment.
Play the demo, give it a go. I would buy this again in a heartbeat.
I love this game a lot. I haven't been able to put it down. It's such a cute game and it's so fun to play. Just make sure you get through the tutorial before you close the game the first time lol.
SUPER Cozy... also made me question my own life several times. 10/10, would existentially crisis again.

I love this game, it is definitely my favorite game of all time and I would absolutely replay it. Unfortunately I am experiencing issues with the game display window being too small, and I am on PC not Mac, and the recommended solution is not working. But overall there have been no other bugs that I experienced, and this only started happening recently. This game has beautiful art, an emotional story, and cozy gameplay. I love the world and the exploration aspect. If you love games where you can explore a large map and have a ton of things to unlock, I definitely recommend.

Great game overall. Emotional subject, obviously. I think the game does a good job of letting you get to know the characters, such that you feel the emotional weight of releasing each spirit into the afterlife. Some reviews point out some of the characters can be annoying, but that's life. These spirits are supposed to be ordinary people, so the game gives you a mix of characters, flaws and all.

I can see how some would find the mechanics repetitive and grindy. Some of the mini-games to collect resources or make materials can get old fast. But then again many games, especially cozy games, have such repetitive mechanics (crafting, cooking, resource gathering). This is a cozy game so such mechanics are to be expected.
8/10 the game was very fun and the story is amazing although it is a bit of a slow burner especially if you don't multi task but maybe thats the fun part of a cozy game, overall though very good.
i love spirit helping games sm, i always get attached and get sad when they leave but theyre so sweet. very nice and chill game :D
the art, characters, music, etc. is all so endearing and sweet it's so cozy

[b]Gorgeous, cozy, and meditative, but constantly undermining itself.[/b]

I [i]wanted[/i] to love this game. It's beautiful, the gameplay is comfy and well polished, it tackles important themes respectfully. But somehow, every single time it tried to build up emotional connections or play out an important scene, events would conspire to undermine it. Quest chains would break, or interractions would bug out, or I'd get stuck for hours. Relatively minor issues, honestly, but [i]consistently[/i] occuring at the worst times to kill any sympathy or interest. Rather than saying farewell to close friends, it felt more like I was apathetically catering to demanding customers.

Also, for a game about death and goodbyes, Spiritfarer doesn't feel like it has much to [i]say[/i] about it. It's more of a series of vignettes. A peek into the lives of very different people, who handle the end in different ways. Maybe that was the goal, but it left me feeling underwhelmed. For every heartwarming or inspiring message, there were just as many contradictory opinions. Maybe the intention is for the player to choose whose outlook they agree with, but I didn't feel like I came away from Spiritfarer having learned anything or changed in any way. No conclusion seemed to be offered other than "Death is inevitable", which is about the least ambitious take possible.

I expected more from that game, it's not a bad game but it's more in the OK+ cathegory.
The french translation wasn't not really polish, the game itself has a few issue but more importantly the naration and the game design are quite average while i was expecting a lot, especially on the naration side of thing.

I think there so many master piece now, and this in not one of them. not a bad game but not ground breaking
This is a fantastic game, that will pull at your heartstrings. The art style is fantastic, the characters are all great, and the management mechanics just elevate the game.

It's an interesting game with hints to some Nintendo games. Although the graphics aren't that glossy, they emphasise things at good points and have their own style. The narrative aspect is very well interwoven in the gameplay, and the tension is tight till the very last moment.

After 44 hours I wasn't prepared to cry like a baby for the ending of the game. I had no idea of the lore of the game and have slowly caught it through the spirit interactions. I cried taking my first spirit through. Not even fully knowing the meaning, but feeling the loss. And then the end hit me like a ton of bricks and the song is a new favorite. Tucked away to remind me of the love I've held that has passed to a world unseen.

It's really novel to see a management life-sim game be purely character focused. With the main goal of the game being to escort spirits to the afterlife, it creates this really interesting formula that makes the motivation to keep playing not personal progression, but through helping others instead. The final scenes you get with each character can be really touching and emotional, which I haven't seen many games accomplish so well.

The game looks absolutely amazing. The character designs feel so expressive and unique, and there's just a certain charm that comes from a lot of the animations for each character, no matter what they're doing. The movement feels WAY too good for what's pretty much a house-builder. The combination of cool looking animations and good movement made it way more fun to explore the new islands and events compared to if it was a more generic, top-down life-sim.

That being said, this game isn't perfect. Because all progression is focused on improving your boat and aiding the passengers, that means the more characters you collect, the less time you have to get attached to each of them. For some characters, I didn't really feel motivated to constantly improve my relationship because I wasn't super attached in the first place. This leads to the game feeling like a collect-a-thon for some spirits you have to help.

Overall, I think that the game is really good. I think its value comes more as an experience rather than the gameplay, and while I personally only resonated with a few of those scenes, it was nice to have a video game give me the feels at all. If you're in a dark place, I could see this game hitting really hard for you.

8/10

(game complete at 31.1 hours of gameplay at time of review, part of my review all my games effort)
(short version)
A cozy management game that will tug on your emotional heart strings.  The hero of this game is the music, often I felt like I was immersed in a Miyazaki film.  Is it perfect? No.  Was it worth the experience? 100% yes, even the frustration.  My only recommendation: Feel free to skip the DLC.  Lastly, $30 is too steep for what you get, wait for a sale.

(long version)
What an insane rollercoaster, read reviews if you want to know more.  Quite frankly the reviewers on this game get it right.  Spiritfarer will have you feel pretty much every emotion you can.  I think the best part is each passenger will probally effect each player differently.  I personally hated Mickey; After reading the reviews, and finishing the game, that's kind of the point though right? Not everyone's going to mesh with you.  I'd love to wax poetic but I'll spare readers that...

The good: Music is beyond great, 100% the hero in this game.  The non-DLC story, it's tight and it just works.  The graphics, yeah... it looks like a flash game but this works for this game adding immersion.  Lastly, the emotions you will absolutely feel.

The bad: My main complaint starts when the DLC seamlessly shifts in.  I say seamless but if you're paying attention you can tell immediately when it does.  IMO the game is stronger without it.  I wish I knew how much more grindy it would be, and had the ability to turn the DLC off.  The XP potion mini-game, can just be deleted... Lastly the cost, I know that this isn't always up to the devs.  $30 unfortunately is pricy, a $20 value. Anything $20 and under is perfectly priced.
Second playthrough on Steam. A stunning, emptional and very engaging piece of art. If you're reading this, buy it.
it's a great game! cozy, beautiful, but sometimes heartbreaking. Cool characters with cool stories. Pretty colors and good music. 25-40 hours for a playthrough.

Aesthetically, Spiritfarer is absolutely gorgeous: it nails a very specific, cozy/sad vibe that’s hard to forget.
The mechanics blend together nicely, and managing all the little tasks on your ship is genuinely engaging for a good while. It reminds me a lot of Animal Crossing and Stardew Valley in that aspect.
That said, over time, the repetition starts to wear thin. Some tasks begin to feel more like busywork than meaningful gameplay, and the pacing slows down as a result.

I played "Spiritfarer" for 6 hours and dropped it. 
Even though the story is nice, the repetite and grindy management makes it annoying and boring. Would be better as a short Visual Novel with some click and point mechanic. 

I'm sad. I really wanted to like it after seeeing the nice visuals, the nice story idea and the good reviews. But it just doesn't fit my taste.

4/10 boring.

The story, art and music are great. 

What's not so great is that the game doesn't respect your time. Everything, from mining to planting to crafting is way more tedious than it needs to be. Everything has to be done by hand and every action has some kind of mini-game. It's fun the first few times but the crafting requirements quickly ramp up. 

I would rather spend more time with the npcs and exploring the world and less time crafting metal sheets or whatever the game requires. I was hoping there would be some means of automating tasks but after 20 hours I've given up
Beautiful art, music, and story.
I wish I could wipe my memory and play it again from scratch. That's the whole review. Just play it.

I've given this game more time than I planned to because I kept thinking it would get better... Spoiler, it didn't. This is not an emotional story based game like the video would have you believe, this is at best a resource management game, barely any story, no tie in, no emotional investment. Bought this on sale and didn't think it was worth it.
Touching and creative. One of the best cozy games I have ever played.
good story line and casual game play!
This game is just painfully good. I don't think I can ever play this again after Alice's depart.
truly a heart wrenching game at times but beautiful for it.
Gods i love the characters the music everything homie
i love this game, helped with my anxiety around the thought of what happens after death !
I don't know man, it feels like I'm playing a mobile game designed by furries.

played it on the switch. played it on the pc. would play it again anywhere else.
truly love the idea of the game. perfect amount of grind vs. cozy builder.

this game just had everything i wanted. it was emotional, relaxing, and had a quest list. it really made the game exactly what i love. i dont like games that are super challenging, so this was right up my alley. it does take a long time to complete, but for me the hours went by so fast.

This game is immersive from start to finish. I love the handwritten art style which reminds me of the classic 2D animated movies. The gameplay can feel a little repetitive at times but I’d still recommend it if you want relaxing and wholesome game that isn’t stressful
